plainMeaning: Honour the whole Jain community, help copy and preserve the scriptures, and spread the glory of the faith — these too are the devotee's lifelong duties, always done as a good teacher guides.
recitation: Saṅghovari bahumāṇo, putthaya-lihaṇaṁ pabhāvaṇā titthe; saḍḍhāṇa kiccam-eaṁ, niccaṁ sugurū-vaeseṇaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: towards / in regard to the Saṅgha (the four-fold community)
wordByWord.etymology: saṅgha (community) + uvari < Skt. upari (upon, towards); a sandhi-joined locative-directional compound.
wordByWord.notes: Object of the bahumāṇa that follows; 'uvari' here is relational ('towards'), not literal 'above'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bahumāna < bahu (much) + māna (honour); Prakrit nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: More than ordinary respect — heartfelt, abundant honour.

wordByWord.gloss: the copying / writing (and so preservation) of scriptural books
wordByWord.etymology: putthaya < Skt. pustaka (book, manuscript) + lihaṇa < Skt. lekhana (writing); nom./acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: A concrete act of jñāna-bhakti; classically commissioning manuscripts, today sponsoring printing or digitisation of śruta.

wordByWord.gloss: prabhāvanā — acts that illuminate and raise the esteem of the tīrtha
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. prabhāvanā < pra-√bhū (to make shine forth, manifest).
wordByWord.notes: Glossed in the preface; used here as the third communal duty.

wordByWord.gloss: of / for the tīrtha (the Jina's order and holy crossing-places)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tīrtha (ford, crossing-place; the four-fold order), Prakrit loc. sg. -e.
wordByWord.notes: Locative read with pabhāvaṇā: 'glorification in / of the tīrtha' — the object the prabhāvanā exalts.

wordByWord.gloss: of the devotees (the śrāvakas / pious lay-followers)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śrāddha (a person of faith, śraddhā) → Prakrit saḍḍha; genitive plural -āṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Genitive governing kicca — 'the duty OF the devotees'; the same word the preface links to śrāvaka.

wordByWord.etymology: kiccaṁ < Skt. kṛtya (what is to be done, duty) + eaṁ < Skt. etat (this); sandhi-joined.
wordByWord.notes: The verse's summative clause; 'eaṁ' gathers up all items named in vv.1–5, not only this verse's three.
Stresses that these are standing, lifelong obligations, not occasional acts.

wordByWord.gloss: by the instruction of a true / good guru
wordByWord.etymology: su (good) + guru (teacher) + vaesa < Skt. upadeśa (instruction); instrumental sg. -eṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: The hermeneutic key (suguru-upadeśa, see preface); instrumental — the means by which every duty is rightly performed.

literalTranslation: High regard towards the Saṅgha; the copying of scriptural books; glorification of the tīrtha — this is the duty of the devotees, [to be done] always by the instruction of a good guru.
idiomaticTranslation: Cherish the whole community with abundant reverence; help write out and preserve the holy books; and let your acts shed lustre on the Jina's order. These, too, are the devotee's standing duties — to be lived out every single day under the guidance of a true teacher.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the previous verse turned wholly inward — calm, discernment, restraint of inflow, watchfulness of speech, compassion to the six life-classes — this closing verse turns the devotee outward again, to the widest circle of all: the living tradition itself. Three acts are named, and they ascend in scope. First, saṅghovari bahumāṇo: not mere politeness toward monks, nuns and fellow laity, but bahu-māna, abundant heartfelt esteem for the four-fold Saṅgha as a whole. Second, putthaya-lihaṇaṁ: the very concrete, almost artisanal duty of getting the scriptures copied and preserved — the householder's share in guarding śruta-jñāna for generations not yet born. Third, pabhāvaṇā titthe: acting so that the tīrtha shines in the world's eyes, raising the order's esteem rather than one's own. Then the poet seals the entire five-verse catalogue: saḍḍhāṇa kiccam-eaṁ — 'this is the devotees' duty,' eaṁ ('this') reaching back to embrace every item from v.1's right faith to v.5's prabhāvanā. Two final words govern everything: niccaṁ, these are constant, daily, lifelong obligations, not festival-day gestures; and sugurū-vaeseṇaṁ, all of them are to be undertaken under a true teacher's instruction. That instrumental phrase is the hinge of the whole sajjhāy — it subordinates the householder's busy, generous, outward-facing life of duty to the discernment of a qualified guru, so that zeal never outruns right understanding.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The three duties here form the outward, tradition-facing tier of the śrāvaka's life and correspond to duties 34–36 of the conventional thirty-six. Pabhāvanā is doctrinally weighty: glorifying the tīrtha is reckoned among the qualities that adorn and strengthen right faith (samyaktva), the householder's counterpart to the renunciant's eight prabhāvakas. Putthaya-lihaṇaṁ is a form of jñāna-bhakti — devotion to knowledge — by which the laity, who cannot themselves transmit the canon as ascetics do, still serve śruta-jñāna materially. Crucially, the verse refuses to leave these as autonomous goods: the instrumental sugurū-vaeseṇaṁ makes guru-upadeśa the operative cause of right action, echoing the Jain conviction that conduct severed from a qualified teacher's guidance risks well-meant harm. Thus the verse both completes the duty-list and re-grounds it in the āṇā (the Jina's command, v.1) mediated through a living preceptor.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today lives this verse by giving generous, non-partisan support to the whole Saṅgha; by funding the printing, translation or digitisation of scriptures and preserving old manuscripts; and by sponsoring or quietly enabling celebrations, pūjās and public works that raise the faith's standing — without seeking personal credit. The closing words are the safeguard: before undertaking such visible 'religious' projects, consult and follow a qualified guru, so generosity serves the tradition rather than the ego.

crossReferences.text: The thirty-six daily duties of a śrāvaka (chattīsa kṛtya), of which respect for the Saṅgha, scripture-copying and prabhāvanā are the last three
crossReferences.source: Śrāddhavidhi-prakaraṇa tradition (Ratnaśekharasūri) and standard śrāvakācāra summaries

crossReferences.text: Prabhāvanā among the practices that adorn and strengthen samyag-darśana

sources.title: Manaha Jinanam Sajjay — Jain Sutras and Stotras (full text, transliteration & 36-duties explanation)
sources.consultedFor: verbatim verse-5 text and the mapping of this verse to śrāvaka duties 34–36 (respect the four-fold Saṅgha; writing/printing of religious books; spreading the message of religion)

sources.title: Can you give the whole Manaha Jinanam sutra in English (full Roman transliteration)
sources.consultedFor: confirming the reading of verse 5 and that putthaya-lihaṇaṁ means copying/preserving scriptural books, not 'sweeping'

sources.title: Mannaha Jinan Aanam Swadhyay — Book Summary (JainGPT)
sources.consultedFor: later printed commentary tradition and the framing of the closing refrain (duties performed by the guru's instruction)

notes: Source-book orthography is preserved verbatim (સંઘોવરિ, પુત્થયલિહણં, સઢ્ઢાણ, સુગુરુવએસેણં); other printed editions show minor variants (e.g. પુત્થય- / પુત્થઅ-, સુગુરુ- / સુગુરૂ-, વએસેણં / વએસેણ). One online search source mis-glossed 'putthaya-lihaṇaṁ' as sweeping a sacred space; this is rejected — the consistently attested sense is pustaka-lekhana, the writing/copying of scriptures, confirmed by both jainknowledge.com pages. 'titthe' is read here as a locative governed by pabhāvaṇā ('glorification of/in the tīrtha'); some readers attach it loosely as 'prabhāvanā [and] the tīrtha,' but the dominant tradition treats prabhāvanā-of-the-tīrtha as a single duty. The pronoun 'eaṁ' (this) summarises the whole five-verse list, not only verse 5's three items.
